title: Superconductivity-enhanced phonon angular momentum
authors: Natsuki Okada, Philipp Werner, Shintaro Hoshino
abstract: We theoretically investigate the properties of phonon angular momentum in the superconducting state, using fulleride compounds in an external magnetic field as a model system. The electron orbital angular momentum injected by an external field is transferred to the phonon subsystem via electron--phonon coupling. We show that this field-induced phonon angular momentum is significantly enhanced and undergoes a sign reversal upon entering the superconducting state. In the normal state, the dominant energy scale governing the response function is the electronic bandwidth $D$. In the superconducting state, the phonon energy scale $ω_1$ enters the denominator, leading to an enhancement of order $D/ω_1$. The observed sign change in the response can be explained by the competition between Fermi surface and Fermi volume contributions.
summary: This paper theoretically demonstrates that superconductivity causes a dramatic enhancement and a sign reversal in phonon angular momentum in fulleride-like systems. It identifies that the enhancement is driven by the ratio of the electronic bandwidth to the phonon frequency. The work provides a new way to understand how orbital angular momentum is transferred from electrons to the lattice in correlated superconductors.
main_problem: The study investigates the mechanism behind the enhancement and sign reversal of phonon angular momentum (PAM) when a system enters the superconducting state under an external magnetic field.
main_result: The researchers show that PAM is significantly enhanced in the superconducting state by a factor of order D/omega_1 and undergoes a sign reversal due to the competition between Fermi surface and Fermi volume contributions.
method: The study uses a theoretical framework combining the local Eliashberg theory with Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) and a spherical tensor formalism to analyze phonon self-energy.
model_or_system: The model system is fulleride compounds (e.g., A3C60) in an external magnetic field, modeled using a three-orbital Hubbard model with p-type fermions and Jahn-Teller phonon modes.
key_observables: Phonon angular momentum (PAM), electronic orbital angular momentum (OAM), superconducting gap function (Delta), and phonon self-energy.
important_parameters: Electronic bandwidth (D), superconducting gap (Delta), phonon frequency (omega_1), transition temperature (Tc), and magnetic field strength (h).
main_assumptions: Assumes a featureless semi-elliptical density of states, neglects direct phonon Zeeman contribution, and uses the BCS limit (no retardation effect) for certain analytical derivations.
figures_summary: Fig 1(a) shows the temperature dependence of the superconducting gap; Fig 1(b) shows the decrease in electronic OAM below Tc; Fig 1(c) illustrates the sign reversal of normalized PAM below Tc.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of PAM in superconductors, describes the Hubbard-Jahn-Teller model and Eliashberg framework, presents the calculation of phonon self-energy and angular momentum, discusses the enhancement and sign reversal mechanisms, and concludes with implications and future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Temperature dependence of (a) the superconducting gap function [inset: bandwidth dependence of the transition temperature] and (b,c) the electronic and phononic orbital angular momenta. In (b) and (c), the vertical axis is normal- ized by the magnitude of the corresponding quantity in the normal state, while the magnitudes are shown in the insets.

FIG. 2. Field-induced phonon self-energy in a weak magnetic field. Circles and triangles correspond to D = 0.3 and D = 0.5, while filled and open symbols indicate the normal and superconducting states.

FIG. 3. Magnetic-field dependence of (a) the electronic angular momenta, OAM and SAM; (b) the phonon angular momentum (PAM); (c) the superconducting pair amplitude, characterized by the orbital and spin angular momenta of the Cooper pairs, (LC, SC); and (d) the electronic spectral function at zero energy. The temperature and bandwidth are T = 0.001 and D = 0.4, respectively.
